title: 100th Digit of (1+sqrt(2))^3000

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Compute the 100th decimal digit of (1 + sqrt(2))^3000.
rules: Direct computation isn't feasible -- the number has thousands of digits.
Use the binomial theorem and the conjugate (1 - sqrt(2))^3000.
setup.question: What is the 100th digit to the right of the decimal point in (1 + sqrt(2))^3000?

learningObjectives: Use the binomial theorem to show (1+sqrt(2))^n + (1-sqrt(2))^n is an integer.
Conclude that (1+sqrt(2))^3000 = N - tiny, so its decimal is N-1.999...9 with many 9s.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: What's the trick to compute one specific digit of an enormous number?

options.text: Add the conjugate (1-sqrt(2))^3000 to (1+sqrt(2))^3000. The sum is an INTEGER (irrational parts cancel by binomial expansion).

stages.explanation: Conjugate-pair trick. By the binomial theorem, (1+sqrt(2))^n = sum C(n,k) sqrt(2)^k and (1-sqrt(2))^n = sum C(n,k) (-sqrt(2))^k. The odd-k terms cancel when added, leaving twice the sum over even k -- which is an integer.

stages.label: Bound the conjugate
stages.prompt: Note that (1 - sqrt(2)) ≈ -0.414, so |1 - sqrt(2)|^3000 = (sqrt(2) - 1)^3000. How large is this?

stages.label: Combine to identify the decimal
stages.prompt: Let N = (1+sqrt(2))^3000 + (1-sqrt(2))^3000 (integer). Then (1+sqrt(2))^3000 = N - (1-sqrt(2))^3000. Since (1-sqrt(2))^3000 is positive (even exponent makes negative -> positive) and < 10^(-100), what does the decimal look like?

options.text: (1+sqrt(2))^3000 = N - epsilon where 0 < epsilon < 10^(-100). In decimal: this is (N-1).999...9 with at least 100 nines after the decimal point.

stages.explanation: (1-sqrt(2))^3000 = ((-1)(sqrt(2)-1))^3000 = (sqrt(2)-1)^3000 (even power makes positive). Subtracting a tiny positive number from integer N gives N - epsilon, which in decimal is (N-1).999...9. The 9s extend far past the 100th place.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: What is the 100th digit after the decimal point?
stages.explanation: The decimal is (N-1).999...9 with 9s extending well past position 100. So digit 100 = 9. Beautiful trick: never compute the actual number.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What is the digit at position 1146 (approximately where the 9s end and other digits begin)?
Generalize: for which n is the 100th digit of (1+sqrt(2))^n equal to 9?
Apply the same technique to (3 + 2*sqrt(2))^1000 -- find a high-precision digit.
